name: epinephrine
indication: Anaphylaxis, cardiac arrest (VF/pVT/asystole/PEA), severe bronchospasm, croup
mechanism: Non-selective alpha and beta adrenergic receptor agonist. Increases HR, BP, bronchodilation.

routes.notes: Cardiac arrest: 1 mg IV/IO q3-5 min. Flush with 20 mL NS after each dose.
Anaphylaxis: inject into anterolateral thigh. EpiPen 0.3 mg adult, 0.15 mg child.
Vasopressor infusion. Titrate to MAP goal. Common mix: 4 mg in 250 mL NS = 16 mcg/mL.
Dilute to 5-10 mL NS. Dose is 2-2.5x IV dose via ETT.

name: adenosine
indication: Paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (PSVT), narrow-complex SVT
mechanism: Transiently blocks AV nodal conduction and slows sinus rate via A1 adenosine receptors.
contraindications: second or third degree AV block

routes.notes: First dose 6 mg rapid IVP, flush with 20 mL NS. Second dose 12 mg. Give via antecubital or central. Half-life ~10 sec.

name: amiodarone
indication: VF/pVT cardiac arrest, stable SVT/VT, atrial fibrillation rate control
mechanism: Prolongs phase 3 action potential, blocks Na/K/Ca channels and alpha/beta receptors.
contraindications: second or third degree AV block

routes.frequency: Cardiac arrest: once; maintenance infusion follows
routes.notes: Cardiac arrest: 300 mg IVP. Post-ROSC: 1 mg/min x6 hr then 0.5 mg/min x18 hr. Dilute in D5W.
Maintenance: 900 mg in 500 mL D5W = 1.8 mg/mL. Requires in-line filter. Monitor for hypotension.

name: atropine
indication: Symptomatic bradycardia, organophosphate poisoning, premedication
mechanism: Competitive antagonist of muscarinic acetylcholine receptors. Increases HR and AV conduction.

routes.notes: Minimum dose 0.1 mg (peds) to avoid paradoxical bradycardia. Adult: 0.5-1 mg IV.
Organophosphate poisoning: 2-4 mg IV/IM q5-10 min until secretions dry.
ETT dose 2-3x IV dose. Dilute in 5-10 mL NS.

name: naloxone
indication: Opioid overdose reversal, respiratory depression from opioids
mechanism: Competitive antagonist at mu, kappa, and delta opioid receptors.

routes.frequency: q2-3min PRN; may repeat or infuse
routes.notes: Half-life shorter than most opioids — monitor for re-narcotization. For non-acute: 0.04 mg titrated.
0.4-2 mg IM for adults.
Narcan nasal spray: 4 mg in one nostril. May repeat in other nostril.
Continuous infusion: 2/3 of effective reversal dose per hour. Monitor closely.

name: morphine
indication: Moderate-to-severe pain, dyspnea in acute pulmonary edema, MI pain
mechanism: Agonist at mu-opioid receptors. Analgesia, sedation, respiratory depression.

routes.notes: Titrate slowly. Assess pain q15 min. Have naloxone available.

name: fentanyl
indication: Moderate-to-severe pain, procedural sedation, RSI adjunct, intubation
mechanism: Synthetic mu-opioid receptor agonist. 100x more potent than morphine.

routes.notes: RSI: 1-3 mcg/kg IV 3 min before induction. Slow push to minimize chest wall rigidity.
Use concentrated solution (50-100 mcg/mL). Limit to 1 mL per nostril.
ICU analgesia infusion. Titrate to pain score.

name: midazolam
indication: Procedural sedation, anxiolysis, seizures, RSI adjunct, alcohol withdrawal
mechanism: Enhances GABA-A receptor activity. Short-acting benzodiazepine.

routes.notes: Procedural sedation: 0.02-0.04 mg/kg IV slow push. Titrate to effect.
Status epilepticus in field: 0.2 mg/kg IN. Max 10 mg (5 mg per nostril).
Rectal midazolam for seizures when no IV access.

name: lorazepam
indication: Status epilepticus, acute agitation, alcohol withdrawal, procedural sedation
mechanism: GABA-A receptor positive allosteric modulator. Intermediate-acting benzodiazepine.

routes.notes: Status epilepticus: 0.1 mg/kg IV (adults: 4 mg, peds: 0.1 mg/kg up to 4 mg). First-line agent.

name: diazepam
indication: Status epilepticus, acute anxiety, muscle relaxant, alcohol withdrawal
mechanism: Long-acting benzodiazepine GABA-A enhancer. Active metabolites prolong effect.

routes.notes: Give slowly (≤5 mg/min). Do not mix with other drugs. Propylene glycol toxicity risk with high doses.
Diastat AcuDial rectal gel for home seizure management.

name: ketamine
indication: Procedural sedation, RSI, pain management, excited delirium, bronchospasm
mechanism: Non-competitive NMDA receptor antagonist. Preserves airway reflexes and respiratory drive.

routes.notes: Dissociative dose: 1-2 mg/kg IV over 1-2 min. Give midazolam 1-2 mg IV to reduce emergence reactions.
Useful when IV access unavailable. Excited delirium: 4-6 mg/kg IM.
Sub-dissociative for pain: 0.3-0.5 mg/kg IV or 1 mg/kg IN.

name: propofol
indication: Induction of anesthesia, ICU sedation, procedural sedation
mechanism: Positive allosteric GABA-A modulator. Rapid onset, short duration due to redistribution.

routes.notes: Lipid emulsion — use dedicated IV line. Monitor for propofol infusion syndrome (PRIS) with prolonged use. Can cause injection site pain.
ICU sedation: start 5 mcg/kg/min, titrate q10min. Max 67 mcg/kg/min. Monitor triglycerides.

name: etomidate
indication: Rapid sequence intubation (RSI) induction, especially in hemodynamically unstable patients
mechanism: Enhances GABA-A receptor. Minimal cardiovascular effects. Suppresses adrenal cortisol.

routes.notes: Give over 30-60 sec. Causes myoclonus (~33%). Single dose safe — transient adrenal suppression 4-8hr. Preferred in trauma/sepsis RSI.

name: succinylcholine
indication: RSI paralysis (ultra-short, fastest onset), laryngospasm
mechanism: Mimics ACh at nicotinic NMJ receptors; sustained depolarization causing flaccid paralysis.
contraindications: malignant hyperthermia personal or family history
personal or family history of pseudocholinesterase deficiency

routes.notes: Fastest onset NMB (45-60 sec). Pediatrics: 2 mg/kg. Causes fasciculations; pre-treat with vecuronium 0.01 mg/kg to reduce. Hyperkalemia risk ≈0.5 mEq/L per dose.
Laryngospasm rescue when IV unavailable.

name: rocuronium
indication: RSI and intubation (high dose), maintenance paralysis, laryngospasm
mechanism: Competitive antagonist at nicotinic ACh receptors at NMJ.

routes.notes: High-dose RSI (1.2 mg/kg): onset ~60 sec, duration ~75 min. Sugammadex reverses fully. Preferred when succinylcholine contraindicated.

name: dopamine
indication: Cardiogenic shock, septic shock (second-line), symptomatic bradycardia
mechanism: Dose-dependent: low=D1 renal; mid=beta-1 inotrope; high=alpha vasoconstriction.

routes.notes: Low (2-5): renal/splanchnic vasodilation. Mid (5-10): inotropic. High (10-20): vasopressor. Common mix: 400 mg in 250 mL D5W = 1.6 mg/mL.

name: norepinephrine
indication: Septic shock (first-line vasopressor), distributive shock, vasodilatory shock
mechanism: Primarily alpha-1 vasoconstriction with some beta-1 inotropic effect.

routes.notes: Sepsis first-line vasopressor. Start 0.01-0.05 mcg/kg/min, titrate to MAP≥65. Central line preferred. Mix: 4 mg in 250 mL D5W = 16 mcg/mL.

name: vasopressin
indication: Refractory vasodilatory shock (adjunct to NE), cardiac arrest (VF/asystole)
mechanism: V1 receptor agonist causing direct vasoconstriction. Catecholamine-independent mechanism.

routes.frequency: Once in cardiac arrest (replaces 1 or 2 epi doses)
routes.notes: Cardiac arrest: 40 units IV/IO once. Not recommended as routine replacement for epinephrine per 2020 AHA.
Shock: fixed rate 0.03-0.04 units/min. Do not titrate — co-titrate NE instead. Mix: 20 units in 100 mL NS = 0.2 units/mL.

name: dobutamine
indication: Cardiogenic shock, acute decompensated heart failure, low cardiac output states
mechanism: Primarily beta-1 agonist. Increases cardiac contractility and output with less vasoconstriction.

routes.notes: Start 2-3 mcg/kg/min, titrate q10-15 min. May cause tachycardia and increase O2 demand. Mix: 250 mg in 250 mL D5W = 1 mg/mL.

name: nitroglycerin
indication: Acute coronary syndrome, hypertensive emergency with ACS, acute pulmonary edema, angina
mechanism: Releases nitric oxide → smooth muscle relaxation → venous then arterial dilation. Reduces preload/afterload.
contraindications: PDE-5 inhibitor use within 24-48 hours (sildenafil, tadalafil)

routes.notes: Sublingual tablet 0.3-0.4 mg. Sit or lie patient down. Check SBP before each dose.
Start 10-20 mcg/min. Titrate by 10-20 mcg/min q5min. Use non-PVC tubing (drug adsorbs to PVC). Common mix: 100 mg in 250 mL D5W = 400 mcg/mL.

name: labetalol
indication: Hypertensive emergency, hypertensive urgency in pregnancy (pre-eclampsia), aortic dissection
mechanism: Combined alpha-1 and beta blockade. Lowers BP without reflex tachycardia.
contraindications: second or third degree AV block

routes.notes: Bolus: 20 mg slow IV over 2 min, then 40-80 mg q10min. Or infusion: 2 mg/min until target BP.
Cumulative max 300 mg. Monitor for bradycardia, bronchospasm.

name: metoprolol
indication: Rate control in atrial fibrillation/flutter, hypertension, acute MI
mechanism: Selective beta-1 antagonist. Reduces HR and myocardial oxygen demand.
contraindications: second or third degree AV block

routes.notes: A-fib rate control: 2.5-5 mg IV q5min x3. Monitor ECG and BP continuously.

name: diltiazem
indication: Rate control in atrial fibrillation/flutter, PSVT, angina
mechanism: Blocks L-type calcium channels in cardiac SA/AV nodes and vascular smooth muscle.
contraindications: second or third degree AV block

routes.frequency: First dose then second dose q15min PRN
routes.notes: First dose: 0.25 mg/kg (max 20 mg) over 2 min. Second dose: 0.35 mg/kg if needed.
Maintenance: 5-15 mg/hr after successful rate control. Mix: 125 mg in 125 mL NS = 1 mg/mL.

name: lidocaine
indication: VT/VF (alternative to amiodarone), local anesthesia, RSI-facilitated (attenuates ICP rise)
mechanism: Blocks fast sodium channels in cardiac tissue. Depresses automaticity.
contraindications: second or third degree AV block

routes.frequency: May repeat 0.5-0.75 mg/kg q5min; max 3 mg/kg
routes.notes: VT: 1-1.5 mg/kg IV bolus. RSI: 1.5 mg/kg IV 3 min before laryngoscopy (attenuates ICP spike). Signs of toxicity: perioral tingling, seizures, cardiovascular collapse.
Dilute to 5-10 mL NS.
Post-conversion VT maintenance. Common: 2 g in 250 mL D5W = 8 mg/mL.

name: magnesium_sulfate
indication: Torsades de Pointes, pre-eclampsia/eclampsia, severe asthma, hypomagnesemia
mechanism: Physiologic calcium antagonist. Membrane stabilizer. Bronchodilation, AV nodal slowing.

routes.notes: TdP: 1-2 g (50% solution) IV push over 15 min. Eclampsia: 4-6 g load over 20 min, then 1-2 g/hr. Monitor: DTRs, respiratory rate, urine output. Antidote: calcium gluconate 1 g IV.
Eclampsia maintenance: 1-2 g/hr. Asthma: 2 g over 20 min infusion.

name: dextrose
indication: Hypoglycemia, altered mental status of unknown etiology (empiric therapy)
mechanism: Rapidly elevates serum glucose. Provides cellular fuel for brain and heart.

routes.notes: Adults: D50W 1 mL/kg (=0.5 g/kg). Peds: D10W 5 mL/kg (=0.5 g/kg) or D25W 2 mL/kg. Check glucose q15 min post-dose. Extravasation causes tissue necrosis — ensure patent IV.

name: albuterol
indication: Acute bronchospasm, asthma exacerbation, COPD exacerbation, anaphylaxis with bronchospasm, hyperkalemia
mechanism: Selective beta-2 receptor agonist. Bronchial smooth muscle relaxation. Also drives K+ intracellular.

routes.notes: Nebulizer: 2.5-5 mg in 3 mL NS. Continuous neb: 10-15 mg/hr. MDI: 4-8 puffs q20min. Monitor HR and K+.
IV route for life-threatening bronchospasm. Hyperkalemia treatment adjunct.

name: ipratropium
indication: COPD exacerbation, acute bronchospasm (adjunct to albuterol), asthma (adjunct)
mechanism: Competitively blocks muscarinic receptors in bronchial smooth muscle. Reduces secretions.

routes.notes: Nebulizer: 0.5 mg (2 mL). Often combined with albuterol in DuoNeb. MDI: 4 puffs q4-6hr.

name: methylprednisolone
indication: Acute asthma/COPD exacerbation, anaphylaxis (secondary), spinal cord injury (controversial), adrenal crisis, inflammatory conditions
mechanism: Glucocorticoid receptor agonist. Anti-inflammatory, immunosuppressive, reduces edema.

routes.notes: Asthma: 125 mg IV or 2 mg/kg peds. SCI: controversial 30 mg/kg load (no longer routinely recommended). Flush well after administration.

name: dexamethasone
indication: Croup, cerebral edema, anaphylaxis (adjunct), COVID-19 severe, adrenal crisis
mechanism: Potent glucocorticoid receptor agonist. 25x more potent than hydrocortisone. No mineralocorticoid effect.

routes.notes: Croup: 0.6 mg/kg IM/IV once (max 10 mg). Cerebral edema: 10 mg IV then 4 mg q6hr. COVID-19: 6 mg IV/PO daily x10 days.

name: diphenhydramine
indication: Allergic reaction, anaphylaxis (adjunct), motion sickness, acute dystonia from antiemetics
mechanism: Competitive H1-receptor antagonist. Also has anticholinergic and sedative properties.

routes.notes: Anaphylaxis adjunct: 25-50 mg IV. Dystonic reaction: 50 mg IV, may repeat in 30 min.

name: ondansetron
indication: Nausea and vomiting (chemotherapy-induced, post-op, opioid-induced, gastroenteritis)
mechanism: Selective 5-HT3 antagonist in GI tract vagal afferents and chemoreceptor trigger zone.

routes.notes: Adult: 4 mg IV over 2-5 min. Dilute before slow IVP. Can prolong QT — obtain ECG if concern.

name: ketorolac
indication: Moderate-to-severe acute pain, renal colic, migraine (adjunct)
mechanism: Non-selective COX-1/COX-2 inhibitor. Reduces prostaglandin synthesis.

routes.notes: Adult: 15-30 mg IV q6hr. Elderly/renal impairment: reduce to 15 mg. Do not exceed 5 days.
Initial IM dose: 60 mg adults, 30 mg elderly/small patients.

name: acetaminophen
indication: Mild-to-moderate pain, fever, adjunct analgesic (opioid-sparing)
mechanism: Central prostaglandin synthesis inhibition. Exact mechanism incompletely understood.

routes.frequency: q6hr; max 4 g/day adults (2 g/day with hepatic risk)
routes.notes: IV acetaminophen (Ofirmev): infuse over 15 min. Adults: 1000 mg. Peds >50 kg: 1000 mg; <50 kg: 15 mg/kg. Max 75 mg/kg/day in peds.
Rectal bioavailability variable (50-80%). Higher doses may be needed.

name: vancomycin
indication: MRSA infections, severe gram-positive infections, C. diff (oral only), empiric coverage
mechanism: Inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis by binding D-Ala-D-Ala peptidoglycan precursor.

routes.notes: Infuse over 60 min (max 10-15 mg/min to avoid Red Man Syndrome). Target AUC/MIC 400-600. Adjust by renal function. Loading dose 25-30 mg/kg in serious infections.

name: ceftriaxone
indication: Pneumonia, meningitis, sepsis, urinary tract infections, STIs (gonorrhea), Lyme disease
mechanism: Inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis by binding penicillin-binding proteins (PBPs).
contraindications: concurrent calcium-containing IV solutions in neonates

routes.notes: Infuse over 30-60 min. Meningitis: 2 g IV q12hr (peds 100 mg/kg/day div q12hr). Do not mix with calcium solutions (precipitate formation).
Gonorrhea: 500 mg IM once. Mix with 1% lidocaine to reduce injection pain.

name: piperacillin_tazobactam
indication: Polymicrobial infections, sepsis (broad-spectrum empiric), pneumonia, intra-abdominal infections
mechanism: Piperacillin inhibits PBPs (broad-spectrum); tazobactam inhibits beta-lactamases.

routes.notes: Extended infusion (4 hr) improves pharmacodynamic target attainment. Adult: 3.375-4.5 g q6-8hr. Adjust for CrCl <40 mL/min.

name: heparin
indication: DVT/PE treatment and prophylaxis, ACS (NSTEMI/STEMI adjunct), AFib anticoagulation, ECMO/CPB
mechanism: Binds antithrombin III, accelerating inactivation of thrombin (IIa) and Xa.

routes.notes: Weight-based protocol: 80 units/kg bolus (max 10,000 units), then 18 units/kg/hr infusion. Monitor anti-Xa or aPTT q6hr. HIT risk: monitor platelets every 2-3 days.
Standard infusion: 25,000 units in 250 mL NS = 100 units/mL. Adjust per hospital heparin nomogram.
DVT prophylaxis: 5000 units SQ q8-12hr. Abdominal adipose tissue preferred site.

name: furosemide
indication: Acute pulmonary edema, congestive heart failure, hypertensive emergency, hyperkalemia, hypercalcemia
mechanism: Inhibits Na-K-2Cl cotransporter in thick ascending Loop of Henle. Increases urine output.

routes.notes: Acute CHF: 40-80 mg IV (or match oral dose). Inject at ≤4 mg/min to reduce ototoxicity risk. Monitor electrolytes, fluid status.
Continuous infusion: 500 mg in 100 mL NS = 5 mg/mL. Titrate to urine output goal.

name: insulin_regular
indication: Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), hyperglycemia, hyperkalemia (with dextrose), HHS
mechanism: Binds insulin receptors; drives glucose/K+ into cells. In DKA: suppresses ketogenesis.

routes.frequency: Loading bolus then infusion; adjust hourly
routes.notes: DKA: 0.1 units/kg/hr infusion (many protocols omit bolus). Target glucose drop 50-75 mg/dL/hr. Hyperkalemia: 10 units IV + D50W 25g simultaneously.
Mix 100 units in 100 mL NS = 1 unit/mL. Flush tubing with 50 mL before connecting (insulin adsorbs to PVC tubing).

name: sodium_bicarbonate
indication: Metabolic acidosis, TCA overdose (cardiac stabilization), hyperkalemia, rhabdomyolysis, sodium channel blocker toxicity
mechanism: Buffers H+ ions. In TCA/Na-channel blocker toxicity: overcomes Na-channel blockade via alkalinization and Na loading.

routes.frequency: Repeat q5-10min as needed; then infusion
routes.notes: TCA/Wide-QRS toxicity: 1-2 mEq/kg bolus until QRS narrows or pH 7.50-7.55. Mix: 150 mEq in 1000 mL D5W for infusion. Monitor Na, pH, K+, Ca2+.

name: calcium_gluconate
indication: Hyperkalemia (cardiac membrane stabilization), hypocalcemia, magnesium toxicity reversal, calcium channel blocker overdose
mechanism: Antagonizes the cardiac and neuromuscular effects of hyperkalemia and magnesium. Does not lower serum K+.

routes.frequency: May repeat in 5 min if no ECG improvement
routes.notes: Hyperkalemia: 1-2 g IV over 5-10 min via peripheral IV. Cardiac effect onset: 1-3 min, duration ~30-60 min. Does not lower serum K+ — use dextrose/insulin concurrently.

name: phenylephrine
indication: Hypotension (non-cardiogenic), vasodilatory shock, SVT termination (reflex bradycardia)
mechanism: Selective alpha-1 agonist. Pure vasoconstriction without inotropic effect. No beta activity.

routes.notes: Bolus for acute hypotension. May use for spinal anesthesia hypotension.
Mix: 100 mg in 250 mL NS = 0.4 mg/mL. Preferred in tachycardia-induced hypotension (does not increase HR).

name: digoxin
indication: Atrial fibrillation rate control (second-line), systolic heart failure (symptom management)
mechanism: Inhibits Na-K ATPase pump → increased intracellular Ca²⁺ → positive inotropy. Also increases vagal tone → rate control.
contraindications: second or third degree AV block

routes.notes: Loading: 0.5 mg IV, then 0.25 mg q6hr x2 (total 1 mg over 24 hr). Check K+ and Mg before dosing. Narrow therapeutic index: 0.5-2 ng/mL. Draw levels 6-8hr post-dose.

name: haloperidol
indication: Acute agitation, delirium, psychosis, Tourette syndrome
mechanism: Blocks D2 dopamine receptors in mesolimbic and mesocortical pathways.

routes.notes: Acute agitation: 2-5 mg IV (0.025-0.1 mg/kg). Monitor QTc. Can combine with lorazepam 2 mg for B52 protocol.

name: adenosine
indication: Paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (PSVT), narrow-complex SVT
mechanism: Transiently blocks AV nodal conduction and slows sinus rate via A1 adenosine receptors.
contraindications: second or third degree AV block

routes.notes: First dose 6 mg rapid IVP, flush with 20 mL NS. Second dose 12 mg. Give via antecubital or central. Half-life ~10 sec.

name: tranexamic_acid
indication: Traumatic hemorrhage (within 3 hours of injury), postpartum hemorrhage, surgical bleeding
mechanism: Competitively inhibits plasminogen activation, preventing fibrinolysis. Preserves clot integrity.

routes.frequency: Loading dose; follow with 1 g maintenance over 8 hr
routes.notes: CRASH-2 protocol: 1 g IV over 10 min (loading), then 1 g over 8 hr. Must give within 3 hours of injury — no benefit after. Dilute in NS or D5W. PPH: 1 g slow IV.

name: alteplase
indication: Ischemic stroke (within 3-4.5 hours), massive PE, STEMI (if PCI unavailable), catheter occlusion
mechanism: Recombinant tPA: converts plasminogen to plasmin, which degrades fibrin clots.
reversal_agent: aminocaproic acid or tranexamic acid for bleeding; cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen

routes.notes: Stroke: 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg): give 10% as IV bolus over 1 min, remainder over 60 min. STEMI (lytics): 15 mg bolus, 0.75 mg/kg over 30 min, 0.5 mg/kg over 60 min. Massive PE: 100 mg over 2 hours.

name: glucagon
indication: Severe hypoglycemia (when IV access unavailable), beta-blocker or calcium channel blocker overdose
mechanism: Activates hepatic glucagon receptors → glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis (raises glucose). In OD: positive chronotropy/inotropy via cAMP, bypassing blocked receptors.

routes.notes: Hypoglycemia: 1 mg IV/IM/SQ adult; 0.5 mg child <20 kg. BB/CCB OD: 5-10 mg IV bolus over 5 min, then 5-10 mg/hr infusion.
Must be reconstituted before use. Position patient on side (vomiting common).

name: activated_charcoal
indication: Oral poisoning/overdose decontamination (within 1-2 hours of ingestion)
mechanism: Large surface area adsorbs toxins in GI tract, preventing systemic absorption.
contraindications: lithium, heavy metals, ethanol — poorly adsorbed

routes.notes: Slurry in water. Adults: 25-50 g. Peds: 1 g/kg up to 25 g. Multi-dose: 0.5 g/kg q4hr for phenobarbital, theophylline, carbamazepine, dapsone, quinine. Not effective for: metals, alcohols, cyanide.
